title: Maximum product subarray
description: Find contiguous subarray with maximum product

examples.explanation: Array: [2, 3, -2, 4]. Initialize max_product = 2. Start i=0: current_product=1. j=0: current_product=1*2=2, max_product=max(2,2)=2. j=1: current_product=2*3=6, max_product=max(2,6)=6. j=2: current_product=6*(-2)=-12, max_product=max(6,-12)=6. j=3: current_product=-12*4=-48, max_product=max(6,-48)=6. i=1: current_product=1. j=1: current_product=1*3=3, max_product=max(6,3)=6. j=2: current_product=3*(-2)=-6, max_product=max(6,-6)=6. j=3: current_product=-6*4=-24, max_product=max(6,-24)=6. i=2: current_product=1. j=2: current_product=1*(-2)=-2, max_product=max(6,-2)=6. j=3: current_product=-2*4=-8, max_product=max(6,-8)=6. i=3: current_product=1. j=3: current_product=1*4=4, max_product=max(6,4)=6. Final max_product = 6.

Array: [-2, 0, -1]. Initialize max_product = -2. Start i=0: current_product=1. j=0: current_product=1*(-2)=-2, max_product=max(-2,-2)=-2. j=1: current_product=-2*0=0, max_product=max(-2,0)=0. j=2: current_product=0*(-1)=0, max_product=max(0,0)=0. i=1: current_product=1. j=1: current_product=1*0=0, max_product=max(0,0)=0. j=2: current_product=0*(-1)=0, max_product=max(0,0)=0. i=2: current_product=1. j=2: current_product=1*(-1)=-1, max_product=max(0,-1)=0. Final max_product = 0.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Check all possible contiguous subarrays by iterating through each starting index and extending to compute products, keeping track of the maximum. Use nested loops to generate subarrays and multiply elements incrementally.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Maximum Product Subarray
ladders.desc: Complete O(n²) solution checking all contiguous subarrays
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the maximum product subarray problem by systematically examining every possible contiguous subarray in the array. The intuition is straightforward: since we need to find the contiguous subarray with the maximum product, we can simply compute the product for every possible subarray and track the maximum value encountered. This exhaustive search guarantees we'll find the optimal solution because we're checking all possibilities. The algorithm uses nested loops: the outer loop iterates through each possible starting index, and the inner loop extends from that starting point to the end of the array. As we extend the subarray, we multiply the current element with the running product from the previous iteration, avoiding redundant calculations. We maintain a variable to track the maximum product seen so far, updating it whenever we find a larger product. The time complexity is O(n²) because for each of n starting indices, we examine up to n ending indices, resulting in roughly n(n+1)/2 subarray checks. Space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few variables regardless of input size. This approach connects directly to the problem statement by providing a complete, correct solution. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle the empty subarray (which has undefined product), not initializing the maximum properly (should start with the first element or negative infinity), and integer overflow with large products. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. If array is empty, return 0 or handle appropriately. 2. Initialize max_product with the first element (or negative infinity for safety). 3. For i from 0 to n-1: a. Initialize current_product = 1. b. For j from i to n-1: i. Multiply current_product by arr[j]. ii. Update max_product = max(max_product, current_product). 4. Return max_product. Real code pattern: def maxProduct(arr): n = len(arr); if n == 0: return 0; max_prod = arr[0]; for i in range(n): prod = 1; for j in range(i, n): prod *= arr[j]; max_prod = max(max_prod, prod); return max_prod.

examples.trace: Array: [2, 3, -2, 4]. Initialize max_product = 2. Start i=0: current_product=1. j=0: current_product=1*2=2, max_product=max(2,2)=2. j=1: current_product=2*3=6, max_product=max(2,6)=6. j=2: current_product=6*(-2)=-12, max_product=max(6,-12)=6. j=3: current_product=-12*4=-48, max_product=max(6,-48)=6. i=1: current_product=1. j=1: current_product=1*3=3, max_product=max(6,3)=6. j=2: current_product=3*(-2)=-6, max_product=max(6,-6)=6. j=3: current_product=-6*4=-24, max_product=max(6,-24)=6. i=2: current_product=1. j=2: current_product=1*(-2)=-2, max_product=max(6,-2)=6. j=3: current_product=-2*4=-8, max_product=max(6,-8)=6. i=3: current_product=1. j=3: current_product=1*4=4, max_product=max(6,4)=6. Final max_product = 6.

Array: [-2, 0, -1]. Initialize max_product = -2. Start i=0: current_product=1. j=0: current_product=1*(-2)=-2, max_product=max(-2,-2)=-2. j=1: current_product=-2*0=0, max_product=max(-2,0)=0. j=2: current_product=0*(-1)=0, max_product=max(0,0)=0. i=1: current_product=1. j=1: current_product=1*0=0, max_product=max(0,0)=0. j=2: current_product=0*(-1)=0, max_product=max(0,0)=0. i=2: current_product=1. j=2: current_product=1*(-1)=-1, max_product=max(0,-1)=0. Final max_product = 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute All Subarray Products from a Starting Index
ladders.desc: Given a starting index, compute products of all subarrays beginning at that index
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core computational engine of the brute force approach. The intuition is that for a fixed starting index, we can efficiently compute products of all subarrays that begin at that position by extending one element at a time and multiplying cumulatively. This avoids recalculating products from scratch for each subarray. The algorithm takes an array and a starting index i as input, then iterates through all possible ending indices j from i to the end of the array. It maintains a running product that gets multiplied by each new element as we extend the subarray. This running product represents the product of the subarray from i to j. We compare this product against a maximum tracker and update it if necessary. The time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n) in the worst case when i=0, as we might iterate through all n elements. Space complexity is O(1) since we use constant extra variables. This feeds into the parent problem (L0) by being the inner loop that's executed for each starting index. Common mistakes include not resetting the running product to 1 for each new starting index, or incorrectly handling the case when the array is empty. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize running_product = 1. 2. Initialize max_from_start = negative infinity (or first element if available). 3. For j from start_index to n-1: a. running_product = running_product * arr[j]. b. max_from_start = max(max_from_start, running_product). 4. Return max_from_start. Real code pattern: def maxProductFromStart(arr, start): n = len(arr); if n == 0: return 0; running = 1; max_val = float('-inf'); for j in range(start, n): running *= arr[j]; max_val = max(max_val, running); return max_val.

examples.trace: Array: [2, 3, -2, 4], start_index=1. Initialize running=1, max_val=-inf. j=1: running=1*3=3, max_val=max(-inf,3)=3. j=2: running=3*(-2)=-6, max_val=max(3,-6)=3. j=3: running=-6*4=-24, max_val=max(3,-24)=3. Wait, correction: The maximum from start 1 is actually 12 from subarray [3, -2, 4]? Let's recalculate carefully: Subarrays from index 1: [3] product=3, [3,-2] product=-6, [3,-2,4] product=-24. So max is 3. But wait, there's also subarray starting at 1 with just [3] giving 3. Yes, max is 3. My initial 12 was wrong. Correct trace: running starts at 1. j=1: running=1*3=3, max_val=3. j=2: running=3*(-2)=-6, max_val=max(3,-6)=3. j=3: running=-6*4=-24, max_val=max(3,-24)=3. Output is 3.

Array: [-1, 2, 3], start_index=0. Initialize running=1, max_val=-inf. j=0: running=1*(-1)=-1, max_val=max(-inf,-1)=-1. j=1: running=-1*2=-2, max_val=max(-1,-2)=-1. j=2: running=-2*3=-6, max_val=max(-1,-6)=-1. Wait, that gives -1, but the maximum product from start 0 should be 6 from subarray [2,3]? No, from start 0, subarrays are: [-1] product=-1, [-1,2] product=-2, [-1,2,3] product=-6. So max is -1. But if we consider subarray [2,3], it doesn't start at 0. So output is -1. Let me recalculate the problem: The sub-routine computes products of all subarrays starting at given index. For start 0 on [-1,2,3], products are: [-1]=-1, [-1,2]=-2, [-1,2,3]=-6. Maximum is -1. So output -1. But wait, the example says output 6, which is wrong. Correct output should be -1. I'll correct: Output is -1. Trace: running=1. j=0: running=1*(-1)=-1, max_val=-1. j=1: running=-1*2=-2, max_val=max(-1,-2)=-1. j=2: running=-2*3=-6, max_val=max(-1,-6)=-1. Final max_val = -1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Subarray Product Computation
ladders.desc: Compute product of a subarray by extending incrementally and multiplying
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on how to efficiently compute the product of a subarray as we extend it element by element. The intuition is that instead of multiplying all elements from scratch for each new subarray, we can use the product from the previous subarray and multiply it by the new element. This incremental approach reduces redundant calculations. For example, if we have computed the product of subarray [i...j], then the product of subarray [i...j+1] is simply product([i...j]) * arr[j+1]. This builds on the observation that subarrays are nested: longer subarrays contain shorter ones starting at the same index. The algorithm involves maintaining a running product that gets updated as we add each new element to the current subarray. We start with a product of 1 (the multiplicative identity) and multiply by each element in sequence. This logic is fundamental to the brute force approach because it makes the O(n²) solution feasible; without it, we'd have O(n³) complexity from recomputing products. Time complexity for computing products of all subarrays from a fixed start is O(n), and space is O(1). This connects to L1 by being the mechanism inside the inner loop. Common mistakes include not initializing the running product to 1, or forgetting that multiplying by zero resets the product chain. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize running_product = 1. 2. For each element in the sequence (from start to end): a. running_product = running_product * current_element. b. Use running_product for further calculations (e.g., compare to max). Real code pattern: running = 1; for elem in subarray: running *= elem; # running now holds product of subarray.

examples.input: Start with product=1, extend with elements: [2, 3, -2]
examples.trace: Initialize running=1. First element 2: running=1*2=2. Second element 3: running=2*3=6. Third element -2: running=6*(-2)=-12. Final product = -12.

examples.input: Start with product=1, extend with elements: [0, 5, 2]
examples.trace: Initialize running=1. First element 0: running=1*0=0. Second element 5: running=0*5=0. Third element 2: running=0*2=0. Final product = 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Generating All Contiguous Subarrays
ladders.desc: Enumerate all possible contiguous subarrays of an array
ladders.explanation: This building block involves systematically listing every contiguous subarray of a given array. The intuition is that any contiguous subarray can be uniquely identified by its starting index i and ending index j, where 0 ≤ i ≤ j < n. To generate all such subarrays, we can use nested loops: the outer loop iterates over all possible starting indices, and the inner loop iterates over all possible ending indices from that start to the end. This ensures we cover every contiguous segment without repetition. For an array of size n, the number of subarrays is n(n+1)/2, which grows quadratically. This concept is essential for the brute force approach because we need to consider each subarray to compute its product. The algorithm doesn't compute products yet; it just identifies the ranges. Time complexity is O(n²) to generate all subarrays, and space is O(1) if we just process indices. This connects to L2 by providing the subarrays over which we'll compute products incrementally. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, or generating non-contiguous subarrays. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Let n = length of array. 2. For i from 0 to n-1: a. For j from i to n-1: i. The subarray is from index i to j inclusive. Real code pattern: n = len(arr); for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): subarray = arr[i:j+1].

examples.trace: n=2. i=0: j=0 -> [a]; j=1 -> [a,b]. i=1: j=1 -> [b]. Total 3 subarrays.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Multiplying a Sequence of Numbers
ladders.desc: Compute the product of a list of numbers by iteratively multiplying
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves calculating the product of a given sequence of numbers. The intuition is that multiplication is associative, so we can multiply numbers in any order, but typically we process them sequentially from left to right. The product of an empty sequence is often defined as 1 (the multiplicative identity), but for subarrays, we usually have at least one element. The algorithm initializes a result variable to 1, then iterates through each number in the sequence, multiplying the result by that number. This accumulates the total product. For example, product of [2, 3, 4] is computed as 1*2=2, 2*3=6, 6*4=24. Time complexity is O(k) for a sequence of length k, and space is O(1). This connects to L3 because once we have a subarray, we need to compute its product. Common mistakes include integer overflow with large numbers, or not handling zero correctly (since zero makes the product zero regardless of other numbers). The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize product = 1. 2. For each number in the sequence: a. product = product * number. 3. Return product. Real code pattern: def product(seq): result = 1; for num in seq: result *= num; return result.

examples.trace: Initialize product=1. First number 2: product=1*2=2. Second number 5: product=2*5=10. Third number 3: product=10*3=30. Final product=30.
Initialize product=1. First number -1: product=1*(-1)=-1. Second number 4: product=-1*4=-4. Third number -2: product=-4*(-2)=8. Final product=8.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Contiguous Subarrays
ladders.desc: Identify what a contiguous subarray is in an array
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the idea of a contiguous subarray, which is a fundamental building block for array problems. A contiguous subarray is a sequence of elements from an array that are consecutive, meaning they appear next to each other without any gaps. For example, in array [a, b, c, d], some contiguous subarrays are [a], [b, c], or [a, b, c, d], but [a, c] is not contiguous because it skips b. The intuition is that contiguous subarrays represent slices or segments of the original array. They are defined by a starting index and an ending index, inclusive. All elements between these indices are part of the subarray. This concept is crucial because many problems, like maximum product subarray, require analyzing these contiguous segments. To understand it, visualize the array as a line of boxes; a contiguous subarray is any connected section of that line. The number of possible contiguous subarrays in an array of size n is n(n+1)/2, which can be derived by choosing start and end points. This connects to L4 because once we identify a subarray, we can perform operations like multiplication on it. Common mistakes include confusing contiguous with non-contiguous subarrays, or misunderstanding that a single element is a valid subarray. The step-by-step identification: 1. Given an array and indices i and j (with i ≤ j). 2. The contiguous subarray includes all elements from index i to j. Real code pattern: subarray = arr[start:end+1] in Python.

examples.trace: Array has elements at indices 0:10, 1:20, 2:30. Subarray [10,30] would include indices 0 and 2, but skips index 1 (20). Since elements are not consecutive, it is not contiguous.

examples.input: Array: [5, 6, 7, 8], list all contiguous subarrays of length 2
examples.trace: Array indices: 0:5, 1:6, 2:7, 3:8. Contiguous subarrays of length 2 start at index 0: [5,6], index 1: [6,7], index 2: [7,8]. These are all consecutive pairs.

testCases.input: Array: [p, q, r, s], is [p,r] contiguous?
Array: [a, b, c, d], list contiguous subarrays starting at index 1

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n)
approaches.summary: Maintain two variables for maximum and minimum product ending at each index to handle negative numbers, updating them based on the current element. Track the global maximum product across all positions.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Product Subarray with Dynamic Programming
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP with two variables to track max/min products ending at each index.
ladders.explanation: The maximum product subarray problem requires finding a contiguous subarray with the largest product. A naive approach would check all subarrays (O(n²)), but we can solve it in O(n) using dynamic programming. The key insight is that when dealing with products, negative numbers can flip signs: a negative product multiplied by another negative becomes positive. Therefore, at each position, we need to track both the maximum and minimum product ending at that index. The minimum product is important because if the current element is negative, multiplying it with the minimum (most negative) product could yield a new maximum. We maintain two variables: max_ending_here and min_ending_here, initialized to the first element. For each subsequent element, we compute three candidates: the element itself, max_ending_here * element, and min_ending_here * element. The new max_ending_here is the maximum of these three, and the new min_ending_here is the minimum. We also track a global max_product variable that updates whenever max_ending_here exceeds it. This handles all cases: positive numbers increase products, negative numbers can flip signs, and zeros reset the products. The algorithm runs in O(n) time and O(1) space, as we only store a few variables. Connection to L1: This level integrates the key subroutine of updating max/min products at each step into a complete traversal. Common mistakes include forgetting to include the element itself as a candidate (which handles cases where previous products are zero or negative and starting fresh is better), or not updating global max correctly. Real code pattern: Initialize max_ending_here = min_ending_here = global_max = nums[0]. Loop from index 1 to end: candidates = [nums[i], max_ending_here * nums[i], min_ending_here * nums[i]]; max_ending_here = max(candidates); min_ending_here = min(candidates); global_max = max(global_max, max_ending_here). Return global_max.

examples.trace: Initialize: max_end = 2, min_end = 2, global_max = 2. i=1 (3): candidates = [3, 2*3=6, 2*3=6] -> max_end=6, min_end=3, global_max=6. i=2 (-2): candidates = [-2, 6*-2=-12, 3*-2=-6] -> max_end=-2, min_end=-12, global_max=6. i=3 (4): candidates = [4, -2*4=-8, -12*4=-48] -> max_end=4, min_end=-48, global_max=6. Result: 6.

Initialize: max_end = -2, min_end = -2, global_max = -2. i=1 (0): candidates = [0, -2*0=0, -2*0=0] -> max_end=0, min_end=0, global_max=0. i=2 (-1): candidates = [-1, 0*-1=0, 0*-1=0] -> max_end=0, min_end=-1, global_max=0. Result: 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Update Max/Min Product Ending at Current Index
ladders.desc: Compute new maximum and minimum product ending at current index given previous max/min and current element.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the DP approach. At each step in the array, we need to determine the best (maximum) and worst (minimum) product that can be achieved by a subarray ending at the current index. Given the current element 'num', the previous maximum product ending at the previous index 'prev_max', and the previous minimum product ending at the previous index 'prev_min', we compute three candidates: 1) 'num' itself (starting a new subarray), 2) 'prev_max * num' (extending the previous max subarray), and 3) 'prev_min * num' (extending the previous min subarray). The new maximum ending here is the maximum of these three candidates, and the new minimum ending here is the minimum. This works because: If 'num' is positive, multiplying with prev_max gives the largest product, and multiplying with prev_min gives the smallest. If 'num' is negative, multiplying with prev_min (which could be negative) might yield a positive product larger than prev_max * num, hence we consider all candidates. If 'num' is zero, all products become zero, and starting fresh with zero is optimal. The intuition is to always consider restarting the subarray at the current element, as it might be better than extending (e.g., if previous products are negative or zero). Time complexity is O(1) per step, space is O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called in each iteration of the main loop to update state. Common mistakes: Not including 'num' as a candidate (fails when previous products are negative and current element is positive, or when zero resets), or confusing max/min updates. Real code pattern: candidates = [num, prev_max * num, prev_min * num]; new_max = max(candidates); new_min = min(candidates).

examples.trace: Candidates: num=-2, prev_max*num=6*-2=-12, prev_min*num=3*-2=-6. Max of [-2, -12, -6] = -2. Min of [-2, -12, -6] = -12. So new_max=-2, new_min=-12.
Candidates: num=-1, prev_max*num=0*-1=0, prev_min*num=0*-1=0. Max of [-1, 0, 0] = 0. Min of [-1, 0, 0] = -1. So new_max=0, new_min=-1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Handling Negative Numbers with Dual Tracking
ladders.desc: Maintain both maximum and minimum products to capture sign flips from negative numbers.
ladders.explanation: The central insight for solving maximum product subarray efficiently is that negative numbers can turn a minimum product into a maximum when multiplied together, due to sign flips. In arrays with mixed signs, a subarray with an even number of negatives yields a positive product, while an odd number yields negative. To handle this dynamically, we track two values at each position: the maximum product of a subarray ending there, and the minimum product (most negative) ending there. The minimum is crucial because if we encounter a negative number later, multiplying it with the minimum (which is negative) gives a positive product that could become the new maximum. For example, in array [2, 3, -2, 4], at index 2 (element -2), the previous max is 6 and min is 3. Multiplying -2 with min (3) gives -6, and with max (6) gives -12, but the element itself (-2) is higher, so max becomes -2 and min becomes -12. Then at index 3 (4), multiplying with min (-12) gives -48, and with max (-2) gives -8, but 4 itself is highest, so max becomes 4. The global max remains 6 from earlier. This dual tracking ensures we never miss opportunities where negatives combine to form large positives. The logic applies recursively: at each step, update max and min based on current element and previous max/min. Time O(1) per step, space O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the update subroutine. Common mistakes: Only tracking max and ignoring min (fails for arrays like [-2, 3, -4] where product 24 comes from min tracking), or not considering the element itself. Real code pattern: Maintain max_ending and min_ending variables, update using candidates as in L1.

examples.input: Array so far: [2, 3], current max=6, min=3, next element=-2
examples.trace: At index 2: current element = -2. Candidates: -2, 6*-2=-12, 3*-2=-6. Max is -2, min is -12. This shows min (3) used in calculation, but result max comes from element itself.

examples.input: Array so far: [-2, 3], current max=3, min=-6, next element=-4
examples.trace: At index 2: current element = -4. Candidates: -4, 3*-4=-12, -6*-4=24. Max is 24 (from min*-4), min is -12. This demonstrates how min (-6) multiplied by negative yields positive max.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Product of Contiguous Subarray
ladders.desc: Calculate product of all elements in a given contiguous subarray.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves computing the product of a sequence of numbers within an array, specified by start and end indices. Given an array 'arr' and indices 'start' and 'end' (inclusive), the product is arr[start] * arr[start+1] * ... * arr[end]. This is fundamental because the maximum product subarray problem seeks the largest such product over all possible contiguous subarrays. To compute it, initialize a variable 'product' to 1. Iterate from start to end, multiplying each element into 'product'. Handle edge cases: if start > end, the subarray is empty (product is undefined, but typically we consider non-empty subarrays, so assume valid indices). If the array contains zeros, the product becomes zero. If it contains negatives, the sign may flip. Time complexity is O(k) where k = end-start+1, space is O(1). Connection to L2: This operation is what we aim to maximize; in DP, we avoid computing all subarrays explicitly by tracking products incrementally. Common mistakes: Not handling zero correctly (product becomes zero and stays zero), or integer overflow (in real code, use large integer types or modular arithmetic if needed). Real code pattern: product = 1; for i in range(start, end+1): product *= arr[i]; return product.

examples.trace: Subarray: [0, 2]. product = 1 * 0 = 0; * 2 = 0. Result: 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Multiply Two Numbers with Sign Handling
ladders.desc: Perform multiplication of two integers, considering how signs affect the result.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves multiplying two integers, a and b, to get a product. The key aspect is sign handling: positive * positive = positive, positive * negative = negative, negative * positive = negative, negative * negative = positive. In the context of maximum product subarray, understanding sign changes is crucial because negative numbers can flip the sign of a product, turning a minimum into a maximum. For example, if we have a current product that is negative (minimum) and multiply by a negative number, the result becomes positive and could be a new maximum. The operation is straightforward: product = a * b. In programming, ensure to use integer types that can handle the range (e.g., in Python, integers are arbitrary-precision). Time O(1), space O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is repeated in a loop to compute subarray products. Common mistakes: Forgetting sign rules (e.g., assuming product is always positive), or overflow in languages with fixed-size integers. Real code pattern: result = a * b.

examples.trace: 3 * -2 = -6. Positive times negative yields negative.
-4 * -5 = 20. Negative times negative yields positive.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Positive and Negative Numbers
ladders.desc: Basic arithmetic with positive and negative integers, focusing on multiplication sign rules.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding positive and negative numbers and how they interact under multiplication. Positive numbers are greater than zero (e.g., 1, 2, 3), negative numbers are less than zero (e.g., -1, -2, -3), and zero is neutral. The sign rules for multiplication are: multiplying two positives gives a positive (e.g., 2 * 3 = 6), multiplying a positive and a negative gives a negative (e.g., 2 * -3 = -6), and multiplying two negatives gives a positive (e.g., -2 * -3 = 6). Multiplying by zero always gives zero, regardless of sign. This foundation is essential for the maximum product subarray problem because products can change sign as we include more elements, especially negatives. For instance, if we have a product that is negative and we multiply by another negative, it becomes positive, which might be the maximum we're looking for. Intuition: Think of negatives as 'flipping' the sign; an even number of negatives in a product results in positive, odd gives negative. This concept is taught in elementary math and is critical for algorithm design involving products. Connection to L4: This rule is applied when multiplying two numbers. Common mistakes: Confusing addition rules with multiplication rules (e.g., thinking negative * negative is negative), or mishandling zero. Real code pattern: In code, numbers are represented as integers, and multiplication uses the * operator, automatically handling signs.

examples.trace: 4 is positive, -2 is negative. Positive times negative equals negative. 4 * -2 = -8.
-3 is negative, -5 is negative. Negative times negative equals positive. -3 * -5 = 15.

approaches.name: Prefix-Suffix Scan O(n)
approaches.summary: Compute products from left to right and right to left, resetting at zeros, and take the maximum from both scans. This leverages the property that maximum product subarray lies in one of these directions.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Product Subarray using Prefix-Suffix Scan
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding contiguous subarray with maximum product using bidirectional scanning approach.
ladders.explanation: The maximum product subarray problem requires finding a contiguous subarray within an integer array that yields the highest product. The bidirectional scanning approach elegantly solves this in O(n) time with O(1) space by leveraging two key insights: 1) The maximum product subarray must start or end at either the beginning, end, or at a zero in the array, and 2) By scanning both left-to-right and right-to-left while resetting at zeros, we capture all possible maximum product candidates. The intuition stems from how negative numbers affect products: when we encounter a negative number, the product flips sign, potentially making a previously small negative product become large positive later. By scanning both directions, we ensure we don't miss maximum products that might be hidden by negative sign flips. The algorithm maintains two running products (prefix and suffix) initialized to 1. For each element from left to right, we multiply it into prefix product, resetting to 1 when encountering 0. Simultaneously, we compute suffix product from right to left. The maximum of all prefix and suffix products encountered gives the answer. Time complexity is O(n) since we traverse the array twice. Space is O(1) as we only store a few variables. This approach avoids the complexity of tracking both minimum and maximum products, making it more intuitive while maintaining optimal efficiency. Common mistakes include forgetting to reset at zeros (which would incorrectly propagate products across zeros) and not considering the case where all numbers are negative (where the maximum might be a single element). The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the complete bidirectional scanning logic described there.

examples.trace:
Array: [2,3,-2,4]
Left-to-right scan (prefix):
Initialize prefix = 1, max_product = -inf
i=0: prefix = 1*2 = 2, max_product = max(-inf,2) = 2
i=1: prefix = 2*3 = 6, max_product = max(2,6) = 6
i=2: prefix = 6*(-2) = -12, max_product = max(6,-12) = 6
i=3: prefix = -12*4 = -48, max_product = max(6,-48) = 6
Right-to-left scan (suffix):
Initialize suffix = 1
i=3: suffix = 1*4 = 4, max_product = max(6,4) = 6
i=2: suffix = 4*(-2) = -8, max_product = max(6,-8) = 6
i=1: suffix = -8*3 = -24, max_product = max(6,-24) = 6
i=0: suffix = -24*2 = -48, max_product = max(6,-48) = 6
Final max_product = 6 (from subarray [2,3])

Array: [-2,0,-1]
Left-to-right scan (prefix):
Initialize prefix = 1, max_product = -inf
i=0: prefix = 1*(-2) = -2, max_product = max(-inf,-2) = -2
i=1: prefix = -2*0 = 0 → RESET prefix = 1, max_product = max(-2,0) = 0
i=2: prefix = 1*(-1) = -1, max_product = max(0,-1) = 0
Right-to-left scan (suffix):
Initialize suffix = 1
i=2: suffix = 1*(-1) = -1, max_product = max(0,-1) = 0
i=1: suffix = -1*0 = 0 → RESET suffix = 1, max_product = max(0,0) = 0
i=0: suffix = 1*(-2) = -2, max_product = max(0,-2) = 0
Final max_product = 0 (from subarray containing just 0)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Bidirectional Product Scanning with Zero Reset
ladders.desc: Implement scanning from both directions while resetting product at zeros to capture maximum product candidates.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the core computational engine of the full solution. The key insight is that by scanning the array both forward and backward while resetting the running product to 1 whenever we encounter a zero, we can capture all possible maximum product subarrays. Why does this work? Consider that any maximum product subarray must either: 1) Start at the beginning of the array, 2) End at the end of the array, or 3) Be bounded by zeros. The forward scan captures subarrays starting at the beginning, while the backward scan captures those ending at the end. The zero reset ensures we don't incorrectly combine products across zero boundaries. The algorithm maintains two variables: prefix product (forward scan) and suffix product (backward scan), both initialized to 1. For each position i from 0 to n-1: multiply current element into prefix, update global maximum with prefix, reset prefix to 1 if element is 0. Simultaneously (or in separate pass), for each position i from n-1 to 0: multiply current element into suffix, update global maximum with suffix, reset suffix to 1 if element is 0. Time complexity is O(n) for the two passes. Space is O(1). The connection to L0 is that this sub-routine provides the complete scanning logic needed for the full solution. Common mistakes include not resetting properly at zeros (must reset to 1, not 0) and forgetting to update maximum after each multiplication. Real code pattern: initialize max_product = -inf, prefix = 1, suffix = 1; for i in range(n): prefix *= nums[i]; max_product = max(max_product, prefix); if nums[i]==0: prefix=1; for i in reversed(range(n)): suffix *= nums[i]; max_product = max(max_product, suffix); if nums[i]==0: suffix=1.

examples.trace:
Forward scan:
prefix=1, max=-inf
i=0: prefix=1*1=1, max=max(-inf,1)=1
i=1: prefix=1*2=2, max=max(1,2)=2
i=2: prefix=2*3=6, max=max(2,6)=6
Backward scan:
suffix=1
i=2: suffix=1*3=3, max=max(6,3)=6
i=1: suffix=3*2=6, max=max(6,6)=6
i=0: suffix=6*1=6, max=max(6,6)=6
Final max=6

Forward scan:
prefix=1, max=-inf
i=0: prefix=1*2=2, max=max(-inf,2)=2
i=1: prefix=2*0=0 → reset prefix=1, max=max(2,0)=2
i=2: prefix=1*3=3, max=max(2,3)=3
Backward scan:
suffix=1
i=2: suffix=1*3=3, max=max(3,3)=3
i=1: suffix=3*0=0 → reset suffix=1, max=max(3,0)=3
i=0: suffix=1*2=2, max=max(3,2)=3
Final max=3

ladders.title: Core Logic — Handling Negative Numbers with Bidirectional Scanning
ladders.desc: Understanding how scanning both directions captures maximum products despite negative sign flips.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central insight that makes bidirectional scanning work: negative numbers cause sign flips in products, and scanning both directions ensures we don't miss maximum products that might be temporarily negative. Consider an array like [2, -3, 4]. The forward scan gives: 2, -6, -24. The backward scan gives: 4, -12, -24. We miss the product 2×-3×4=-24? Wait, actually the maximum here is 4. Let's trace properly: Forward: prefix=2 (max=2), then 2×-3=-6 (max=2), then -6×4=-24 (max=2). Backward: suffix=4 (max=4), then 4×-3=-12 (max=4), then -12×2=-24 (max=4). So we correctly get max=4. The key is that when negative numbers create sign flips, the maximum product might be in a subarray that doesn't start at the beginning. The backward scan catches subarrays ending at the end. The real power comes when combined with zero reset: zeros act as boundaries, so we only need to consider segments between zeros. Within each segment, the maximum product is either the product of the entire segment (if it has even number of negatives) or the maximum of: product from start to last negative, or product from first negative to end. The bidirectional scanning implicitly computes these possibilities. Time O(n), space O(1). Connection to L1: this logic explains why the bidirectional scanning in L1 works. Common mistakes: thinking the approach doesn't work for odd negative counts (it does, due to bidirectional scanning). Real pattern: the algorithm naturally handles negatives without explicit tracking.

examples.trace:
Forward scan with zero reset (no zeros here):
prefix=1, max=-inf
i=0: prefix=1*2=2, max=2
i=1: prefix=2*(-3)=-6, max=2
i=2: prefix=-6*4=-24, max=2
Backward scan:
suffix=1
i=2: suffix=1*4=4, max=4
i=1: suffix=4*(-3)=-12, max=4
i=0: suffix=-12*2=-24, max=4
Maximum is 4 from backward scan catching subarray [4]

Forward scan:
prefix=1, max=-inf
i=0: prefix=1*(-2)=-2, max=-2
i=1: prefix=-2*3=-6, max=-2
i=2: prefix=-6*(-4)=24, max=24
Backward scan:
suffix=1
i=2: suffix=1*(-4)=-4, max=24
i=1: suffix=-4*3=-12, max=24
i=0: suffix=-12*(-2)=24, max=24
Maximum is 24 from entire array [-2,3,-4]

ladders.title: Building Block — Running Product with Zero Reset
ladders.desc: Compute running product of array elements, resetting to 1 when encountering zero.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves maintaining a running product while traversing an array, with special handling for zeros. The running product starts at 1 (multiplicative identity). For each element, we multiply it into the running product. When we encounter a zero, we reset the running product to 1 because zero times anything is zero, and continuing would incorrectly propagate products across the zero boundary. This reset is crucial because zeros act as natural boundaries for product subarrays—no maximum product subarray can include a zero (unless the product is zero itself, handled separately). The intuition: if we have array [a,b,0,c,d], the maximum product subarray is either in [a,b] segment, [c,d] segment, or just [0]. By resetting at zero, we ensure we consider each segment independently. Time O(n), space O(1). Connection to L2: this zero-reset mechanism enables handling array segments between zeros, which is essential for the bidirectional scanning logic. Common mistakes: resetting to 0 instead of 1 (would make all subsequent products zero), or not resetting at all. Real code: running_product = 1; for num in array: running_product *= num; if num == 0: running_product = 1.

examples.trace:
Initialize running=1
Process 2: running=1*2=2
Process 3: running=2*3=6
Process 0: running=6*0=0 → reset to 1
Process 4: running=1*4=4
Process 5: running=4*5=20
Final running products tracked: [2,6,1,4,20]

Initialize running=1
Process 1: running=1*1=1
Process 0: running=1*0=0 → reset to 1
Process 2: running=1*2=2
Process 0: running=2*0=0 → reset to 1
Process 3: running=1*3=3
Final running products: [1,1,2,1,3]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Traversal and Multiplication
ladders.desc: Iterate through array elements and perform multiplication operations.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves two key skills: 1) Traversing an array from start to end (and potentially in reverse), and 2) Performing multiplication operations on numeric elements. Array traversal is typically done with a loop (for or while) that accesses each element by index. Multiplication is the arithmetic operation of combining numbers. In programming, we need to handle edge cases like empty arrays (no traversal needed) and integer overflow (though often not considered in problem constraints). The intuition for this operation in the context of maximum product subarray is that we need to examine every element to compute products of contiguous segments. Time complexity for traversing n elements is O(n). Space is O(1) if we only use a few variables. Connection to L3: this basic traversal and multiplication enables the running product computation. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in loop bounds, forgetting to initialize product variable (should be 1, not 0), or not handling sign correctly in multiplication. Real code: product = 1; for i in range(len(array)): product = product * array[i].

examples.trace:
Initialize product=1
Access element 0: value=2, product=1*2=2
Access element 1: value=3, product=2*3=6
Access element 2: value=4, product=6*4=24
Final product=24

Initialize product=1
Access element 0: value=-1, product=1*(-1)=-1
Access element 1: value=5, product=-1*5=-5
Final product=-5

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Contiguous Subarrays
ladders.desc: Identify what arrays are and what constitutes a contiguous subarray.
ladders.explanation: An array is a fundamental data structure consisting of a collection of elements, each identified by an index. In programming, arrays are typically zero-indexed (first element at index 0). A contiguous subarray is a sequence of elements from the array that appear consecutively without gaps. For example, in array [a,b,c,d], some contiguous subarrays are: [a], [a,b], [b,c], [c,d], [a,b,c], [b,c,d], [a,b,c,d]. Non-contiguous selections like [a,c] are not subarrays. The intuition for the maximum product problem is that we need to find one such contiguous subarray that maximizes the product of its elements. This foundation is crucial because the problem constraint 'contiguous' significantly reduces the search space compared to all possible subsets. Time/space: just understanding concepts, no computation. Connection to L4: once we understand arrays and subarrays, we can traverse them to compute products. Common mistakes: confusing subarrays with subsequences (which don't require contiguity) or subsets. Real pattern: a subarray from index i to j includes all elements from i to j inclusive.

examples.trace:
Start with array of length 3.
Subarrays of length 1: from index 0: [1]; index 1: [2]; index 2: [3].
Subarrays of length 2: from index 0-1: [1,2]; index 1-2: [2,3].
Subarray of length 3: from index 0-2: [1,2,3].
Total 6 contiguous subarrays.

Array has only one element.
Only subarray is the entire array itself: [5].
Total 1 contiguous subarray.
